course_info.title: British Literature II
course_info.meeting_schedule: Monday, Wednesday, Friday - 3 hours per week

class_schedule.topic: Romanticism: Revolution, Nature, and the Imagination
learning_objectives: Identify the core values of British Romanticism: imagination, nature, individual experience, and revolution
Analyze Wordsworth's theory of poetry as 'the spontaneous overflow of powerful feelings'
Understand Romanticism as a reaction against Enlightenment rationalism and industrialization
readings: Norton Anthology of English Literature, Romantic period introduction

lecture_notes.introduction: Romanticism transformed English literature by placing individual feeling, the natural world, and the power of imagination at the center of literary value. It was both a literary movement and a cultural response to revolution, industrialization, and the limits of reason.
key_concepts: Wordsworth's poetic theory: language of common speech, incidents of common life, emotion recollected in tranquility
The Romantic imagination as a creative, not merely reproductive, faculty
Nature as teacher, healer, and moral guide in Romantic poetry
The sublime: experiences of awe and terror that overwhelm rational comprehension
The French Revolution as inspiration and disillusionment for the first-generation Romantics
lecture_notes.demonstration: Close reading of 'Tintern Abbey,' tracing how Wordsworth constructs a three-stage relationship with nature: childhood sensory immersion, youthful passion, and mature philosophical reflection
setup_requirements: Norton Anthology of English Literature, Vol. D
Historical timeline of the Romantic period

review.value: An experience of overwhelming magnitude — vast landscapes, violent storms, infinite space — that simultaneously terrifies and exalts, pushing the mind beyond its ordinary limits
Wordsworth's claim that poetry originates not in the immediate experience of emotion but in the later calm reflection upon it, when the poet can shape feeling into art

class_schedule.topic: The Second Generation: Keats, Shelley, and Byron
learning_objectives: Compare the aesthetic philosophies of Keats, Shelley, and Byron
Analyze Keats's concept of 'negative capability' and its implications for poetic practice
Understand the Byronic hero as a cultural phenomenon
readings: Keats, 'Ode on a Grecian Urn' and 'Ode to a Nightingale'

lecture_notes.introduction: The second generation of Romantics — all dead before forty — pushed Romantic ideals in divergent directions. Keats pursued sensuous beauty and philosophical uncertainty. Shelley championed political revolution and the poet as legislator. Byron invented the charismatic anti-hero and satirized everything, including Romanticism itself.
key_concepts: Negative capability: the ability to remain in uncertainty without reaching after fact and reason
Keats's odes: the tension between the permanence of art and the transience of life
Shelley's Defence of Poetry: poets as the unacknowledged legislators of the world
The Byronic hero: proud, rebellious, haunted by secret guilt, irresistible
Romantic irony: Byron's self-aware, self-mocking narrative stance in Don Juan
lecture_notes.demonstration: Comparative reading of Keats's 'Ode on a Grecian Urn' and Shelley's 'Ozymandias,' both addressing the relationship between art and time but reaching opposite conclusions
setup_requirements: Printed copies of all assigned poems
Biographical timeline of the second-generation Romantics

review.value: Keats's term for the quality that Shakespeare possessed: being capable of remaining in doubt and mystery without any irritable reaching after fact and reason
A proud, moody, rebellious figure, defiant of social norms and haunted by past transgressions, who fascinated the Romantic public and influenced literary character types for two centuries

class_schedule.topic: The Victorian Novel: Dickens and Social Realism
learning_objectives: Identify the features of Victorian realism: detailed social observation, moral seriousness, narrative omniscience
Analyze Dickens's use of caricature, sentimentality, and social critique
Understand the Victorian novel's role as a vehicle for public debate about poverty, class, and reform

lecture_notes.introduction: The Victorian novel was the dominant literary form of the nineteenth century and the closest thing to a mass medium before cinema. Dickens used its enormous reach to dramatize social injustice, creating characters so vivid they entered the language as common nouns.
key_concepts: Victorian realism: the conviction that fiction should represent society accurately and address real social problems
Serial publication: how monthly installments shaped narrative structure and audience engagement
Dickensian characterization: the tension between caricature and psychological depth
Class and social mobility in Victorian fiction: the self-made man, the fallen woman, the deserving poor
The bildungsroman: the novel of education and development, which Great Expectations both follows and subverts
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analysis of the opening of Great Expectations — Pip in the graveyard — examining how Dickens establishes character, setting, class dynamics, and thematic concerns in a single scene

review.value: A novel tracing a character's moral and psychological development from youth to maturity, typically involving disillusionment with early ideals
Victorian novels were often published in weekly or monthly installments, creating cliffhangers, encouraging episodic structure, and allowing authors to respond to reader feedback in real time

class_schedule.topic: Victorian Poetry: Tennyson, Browning, and the Dramatic Monologue
learning_objectives: Analyze the dramatic monologue as a poetic form: speaker, auditor, dramatic situation, and revelation
Identify Tennyson's characteristic elegiac mode and its relationship to Victorian doubt
Understand how Browning's dramatic monologues create morally complex speakers the reader must judge
readings: Tennyson, In Memoriam A.H.H. (selections: Prologue, Sections 1, 7, 54, 56, 95, 130)

lecture_notes.introduction: Victorian poetry confronted a crisis of faith. Darwin, geology, and biblical criticism shattered old certainties. Tennyson mourned them in the greatest English elegy. Browning, by contrast, created psychologically complex speakers whose moral failures the reader must detect without authorial guidance.
key_concepts: The dramatic monologue: a speaker addresses a silent auditor in a specific situation, revealing more than they intend
In Memoriam: the poem as a record of grief evolving through doubt toward a fragile faith
Tennyson's engagement with science: 'Nature, red in tooth and claw' and the crisis of natural theology
Browning's method: the reader must judge the speaker without the poet's moral commentary
The Duke in 'My Last Duchess': a masterpiece of unintentional self-revelation
lecture_notes.demonstration: Close reading of 'My Last Duchess,' tracing how the Duke's every attempt to justify himself reveals his possessiveness, jealousy, and implied murder
setup_requirements: Printed copies of all assigned poems
Victorian cultural context: the crisis of faith

review.value: A poem in which a single speaker addresses a silent auditor in a specific dramatic situation, revealing character and often exposing the gap between the speaker's self-image and reality
The intellectual and emotional crisis precipitated by geology, evolution, and biblical criticism, which undermined traditional religious certainty and pervades Victorian literature

learning_objectives: Identify the formal innovations of literary modernism: fragmentation, stream of consciousness, allusion, and difficulty
Analyze T.S. Eliot's The Waste Land as a response to World War I and cultural collapse
Understand modernism's paradoxical relationship to tradition: destroying old forms while obsessively quoting them
readings: Eliot, The Waste Land (complete with notes)

lecture_notes.introduction: Modernism shattered the conventions of Victorian literature. After the carnage of World War I, the ordered, coherent, morally confident narratives of the nineteenth century seemed impossible. Modernist writers responded with fragmentation, difficulty, and a desperate attempt to shore ruins against collapse.
key_concepts: Make it new: Pound's command and the modernist imperative to break with inherited forms
The Waste Land as collage: fragments of myth, literature, and overheard speech assembled without traditional narrative connection
Stream of consciousness: Woolf's and Joyce's attempts to represent the mind's actual flow
The mythical method: Eliot's use of myth to give shape and significance to contemporary chaos
Modernist difficulty: why these writers deliberately made their work hard to read
lecture_notes.demonstration: Guided reading of The Waste Land, Part I ('The Burial of the Dead'), identifying the poem's shifts in voice, language, and allusion and discussing what holds the fragments together
setup_requirements: The Waste Land with annotations (Norton Critical Edition recommended)
Allusion guide handout for Part I

review.value: Eliot's term for using ancient myths as a structural framework for modern content, giving order and significance to 'the immense panorama of futility and anarchy which is contemporary history'
A narrative technique that attempts to render the continuous flow of a character's thoughts, sensations, and associations, abandoning conventional plot and narrative logic

questions.question: What does Keats mean by 'negative capability'? How does this concept apply to 'Ode on a Grecian Urn,' particularly in its final lines?
questions.answer: Negative capability is the capacity to remain in uncertainty and doubt without anxiously seeking resolution. In 'Ode on a Grecian Urn,' the poem circles around unanswerable questions about the figures on the urn, and the famous conclusion — 'Beauty is truth, truth beauty' — may itself be an expression of negative capability: a statement that resolves nothing but holds contradictions in balance.

rubric.key: Connects concept to specific elements of the ode
Engages with the ambiguity of the poem's conclusion

questions.question: Explain how Wordsworth's 'Tintern Abbey' presents nature as having a moral and spiritual influence on the individual. Use specific evidence from the poem.
questions.answer: Wordsworth describes nature's influence as evolving through three stages: childhood sensory pleasure ('Their colours and their forms, were then to me / An appetite'), youthful passionate attachment, and mature philosophical understanding in which nature teaches 'the still, sad music of humanity' and reveals 'a sense sublime / Of something far more deeply interfused.' Nature is not merely scenery but a moral and spiritual presence that shapes the individual over a lifetime.

rubric.key: Identifies nature's moral and spiritual role, not just aesthetic
References specific language or passages from the poem
Describes the developmental progression across stages

questions.question: How does the Duke in 'My Last Duchess' reveal his character through what he intends to be a persuasive self-presentation? Identify two specific moments where the Duke says more than he means to.
questions.answer: First, the Duke's complaint that the Duchess 'liked whate'er / She looked on, and her looks went everywhere' reveals his jealousy and desire for exclusive ownership of her attention — he frames her warmth as a moral failing. Second, his chilling statement 'I gave commands; / Then all smiles stopped together' reveals, beneath its euphemism, that he had her killed — and he mentions it casually to impress an envoy, suggesting he sees murder as a reasonable exercise of aristocratic authority.

rubric.key: First moment accurately identified with textual evidence
Second moment accurately identified with textual evidence
Explains the gap between the Duke's intention and his revelation

questions.question: What is a bildungsroman, and how does Great Expectations both follow and subvert the conventions of the genre?
questions.answer: A bildungsroman is a novel tracing a protagonist's moral and psychological development from youth to maturity. Great Expectations follows the convention by tracing Pip's journey from humble origins through social ambition to self-knowledge. However, it subverts the genre by making Pip's social rise a moral decline: his 'great expectations' make him a snob, and true maturity comes not from success but from the shattering of his illusions about wealth, class, and his benefactor's identity.

rubric.key: Explains how the novel follows the convention
Explains how the novel subverts it through Pip's disillusionment

major_assignments.title: Comparative Period Essay
major_assignments.description: Write a 2,000-2,500 word essay comparing how two texts from different periods (Romantic, Victorian, or Modernist) address a shared theme or formal problem. The essay must analyze how historical context shapes each text's treatment of the theme.
objectives: Demonstrate the ability to compare texts across literary periods
Analyze how historical and cultural context shapes literary form and theme
Construct a comparative argument that illuminates both texts
Integrate at least two secondary scholarly sources
deliverables: 2,000-2,500 word comparative essay in MLA format
Works Cited page with primary texts and at least 2 scholarly sources

criteria: Thesis makes a specific claim about how period context shapes treatment of the theme
Comparison illuminates both texts rather than privileging one
Historical context is specific and well-sourced, not vague generalization
Argument develops logically across the essay
Both texts receive detailed close reading with specific textual evidence
Formal analysis addresses how each text's literary techniques serve its thematic purpose
Analysis goes beyond summary to interpretation
Secondary sources enhance rather than replace the student's analysis
Prose is clear, precise, and well-organized
MLA format is followed correctly throughout
Transitions between texts and periods are smooth
Essay meets length requirements without padding
Insightful comparative argument connecting texts across periods
Detailed close reading of both texts with specific evidence
Effective use of historical context and scholarly sources
Clear, well-organized academic prose in correct MLA format

comparative_essay.description: Comparative essay analyzing texts across two literary periods

reading_responses.description: Weekly close reading responses demonstrating engagement with assigned texts

quizzes.description: Quizzes on literary history, terminology, and assigned readings

midterm_examination.description: Passage identification and analysis covering Romantic and early Victorian periods

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam covering all periods with passage analysis and comparative essay

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory. More than 3 unexcused absences result in a letter grade deduction. Active participation in discussion is expected.
course_policies.late_submissions: Essays are penalized 5% per day late. Reading responses are not accepted late. Extensions require 48 hours advance arrangement.
course_policies.collaboration: All written work must be original individual work. Class discussion is collaborative; written submissions are independent.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Plagiarism, including uncited paraphrase and AI-generated text, will result in a zero and referral to the academic integrity board.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Norton Anthology of English Literature (Volume D recommended), word processing software, library database access, and LMS access.
